samples.claim: For month-to-month tenancies, California requires **30-day notice** if the tenant has resided less than one year, and **60-day notice** if the tenant has resided one year or more (Eviction in the Unit

Landlords must have a just cause — either at-fault (nonpayment, lease violation, nuisance) or no-fault (owner move-in, Ellis Act withdrawal, substantial remodel) — to terminate a tenancy (Just cause e
The **Ellis Act** (Ellis Act) allows landlords to evict tenants to exit the rental business entirely, but requires 120 days' notice (1 year for seniors/disabled).
Los Angeles's Rent Stabilization Ordinance (RSO) requires a 3-day notice followed by a formal unlawful detainer lawsuit (Eviction).
For month-to-month tenancies, a **30-day written notice** is required to terminate the tenancy (Eviction in the United States).
The **Tenant right to counsel** (Tenant right to counsel) law in NYC provides free legal representation to low-income tenants facing eviction.
For month-to-month tenancies, a **30-day notice** is required to terminate the tenancy (Eviction in the United States).
The **Texas Property Code** (Texas Property Code — note: the actual Texas Property Code article is separate) governs landlord-tenant relationships.
Landlords must return the deposit within **21 days** of tenant move-out, with an itemized statement of deductions (Security deposit — note: the actual Security Deposit article is separate).

The **implied warranty of habitability** (Implied warranty of habitability) requires landlords to maintain premises that are fit for human occupation, including working plumbing, heating, electricity,
The **Warranty of Habitability** (Implied warranty of habitability) requires landlords to maintain the premises in "reasonable repair" and fit for human habitation.

It only applies to conditions that "materially affect the physical health or safety of an ordinary tenant." The **implied warranty of habitability** (Implied warranty of habitability) is recognized in
For most residential units, annual rent increases are capped at **5% plus the local Consumer Price Index (CPI)**, with a **maximum of 10%** total increase per year (Rent control).
The **Rent Stabilization Law** and **Rent Control Law** govern approximately 1 million apartments in New York City and select counties (Rent control in New York).
The **statute of limitations** (Statute of limitations) for a landlord to sue for unpaid rent is **4 years** in California.

verdicts: Are exactly 3 states defined (CA / NY / TX)?
Are exactly 5 dimensions enumerated (eviction notice / deposit / repair-deduct / rent-control / lease-break)?
Is a 3 x 5 comparison matrix presented with all 15 cells populated?
Does each cell cite a state-specific statute or a wiki URL?
Does each cell cite >= 1 reddit thread with a real tenant-renter case?
Does each cell cite >= 1 shopping product (book) URL?
Are >= 10 shopping product pages enumerated with price + rating?
Are >= 30 reddit threads cited from at least 4 legal/state sub-forums?
Are all 10 mandatory wiki articles (Landlord-tenant law, Eviction, Security deposit, Rent control, RC-CA, RC-NY, TX Property Code, Lease, Implied warranty of habitability, SoL) cited?
Are exactly 3 legal-aid resources listed per state (9 total)?
Does each legal-aid resource cite a wiki or reddit URL?
Does the matrix explicitly cite state statute names (e.g. CA CIV § 1950.5)?
Are at least 3 cross-state divergences highlighted (e.g. CA 60-day notice vs TX 3-day)?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and sandbox-resolvable?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 10 shopping, >= 30 reddit, >= 25 wiki?
Does the report avoid generic Top-N format?
Does the report avoid chain-of-thought and start with the matrix?
Does the report explicitly disclaim 'not legal advice; consult attorney'?
